days.title: Day 1: Basic Greetings

words.example_sentence_target: Sí, hablo un poco de español.
words.example_sentence_native: Yes, I speak a little Spanish.

dialogues.context: Saying goodbye at the end of the day.

days.cultural_note: In Spain and much of Latin America, greeting people with 'Buenos días' (before noon) or 'Buenas tardes' (afternoon) is common even with strangers, like in shops or elevators. It's considered polite, not overly formal.
days.pronunciation_tip: Spanish vowels are always pronounced the same way, unlike English — 'a' is always 'ah', 'e' is always 'eh'. This makes Spanish words very predictable to read once you learn the five vowel sounds.

days.title: Day 2: Introducing Yourself

words.example_sentence_native: My name is Ana. And you?

sentences.native: I am from Sri Lanka. And you?
sentences.phonetic: සොයි දේ ශ්‍රී ලංකා. ඊ තූ?

sentences.native: Excuse me, sir, what time is it?
I don't speak Spanish very well.
I don't understand, can you repeat?

dialogues.context: Meeting someone for the first time.

lines.speaker: B
lines.target: Hola Carlos. Me llamo María. Mucho gusto.
lines.native: Hello Carlos. My name is María. Nice to meet you.
lines.phonetic: OH-lah KAR-lohs. meh YAH-moh mah-REE-ah. MOO-choh GOOS-toh

lines.speaker: B
lines.target: Sí, hablo un poco. No entiendo todo.
lines.native: Yes, a little. I don't understand everything.
lines.phonetic: see, AH-bloh oon POH-koh. no en-TYEHN-doh TOH-doh

days.cultural_note: In Spanish, 'señor' and 'señora' are respectful ways to address someone whose name you don't know, similar to 'sir' and 'ma'am' — very useful when asking for help from strangers or officials.
days.pronunciation_tip: The Spanish 'll' (as in 'llamo') sounds like the 'y' in 'yes' in most Spanish-speaking regions — so 'me llamo' sounds like 'meh YAH-moh', not 'me LAH-moh'.

days.title: Day 3: Asking Someone's Name

words.example_sentence_native: What is your name? My name is Ana.
Excuse me, I didn't mean to bother you.

sentences.native: Sorry, I didn't hear your name.
Excuse me, are you Mr. García?
sentences.phonetic: දිස්-කුල්-පේ, එස් උස්-තෙද් එල් සෙ-න්‍යෝර් ගාර්-සී-ආ?

lines.speaker: B
lines.native: My name is Sofía. And you?

lines.speaker: B
lines.target: Hola Lakshmi, mucho gusto. Mi nombre es Javier.
lines.native: Hello Lakshmi, nice to meet you. My name is Javier.
lines.phonetic: OH-lah LAHK-shmee, MOO-choh GOOS-toh. meh YAH-moh hah-vee-EHR

dialogues.context: Asking someone to repeat their name.

lines.speaker: B
lines.native: Sorry, can you repeat your name?

days.cultural_note: 'Perdón' and 'Disculpe' are both used like 'excuse me' or 'sorry', but 'Disculpe' is slightly more formal — good to use with officials, older people, or in professional settings.
days.pronunciation_tip: Spanish stress usually falls on the second-to-last syllable of a word, unless there's a written accent mark (like in 'cómo') telling you otherwise — that accent tells you exactly where to stress.

days.title: Day 4: Nationality and Country

words.example_sentence_target: ¿De dónde eres? Soy de la India.
words.example_sentence_native: Where are you from? I'm from India.

words.example_sentence_target: Sri Lanka es un país hermoso.
words.example_sentence_native: Sri Lanka is a beautiful country.

words.example_sentence_target: Vivo en la ciudad de Madrid.
words.example_sentence_native: I live in the city of Madrid.

sentences.target: ¿De dónde eres? Soy de Sri Lanka.
sentences.native: Where are you from? I'm from Sri Lanka.
sentences.phonetic: දේ දොන්-දේ එ-රෙස්? සොයි දේ ශ්‍රී ලංකා.

sentences.target: España es un país en Europa.
sentences.native: Spain is a country in Europe.
sentences.phonetic: එස්-පා-න්‍යා එස් උන් පා-ඊස් එන් එ-උ-රෝ-පා.

sentences.native: I live here, in this city.

lines.speaker: B
lines.target: Soy de Sri Lanka. Es un país en Asia.
lines.native: I'm from Sri Lanka. It's a country in Asia.
lines.phonetic: soy deh sree LAHN-kah. ehs oon pah-EES ehn AH-syah

lines.speaker: A
lines.native: Do you live here in Madrid?

lines.speaker: B
lines.native: Yes, I live in this city.

lines.speaker: A
lines.target: Perdón, ¿dónde está la estación de tren?
lines.native: Excuse me, where is the train station?
lines.phonetic: pehr-DOHN, DOHN-deh ehs-TAH lah ehs-tah-SYOHN deh TREHN

lines.speaker: A
lines.native: Nice to meet you, I'm Carmen.

days.cultural_note: When talking about where you're from, Spanish speakers often ask about your country and city separately — knowing both 'país' (country) and 'ciudad' (city) helps you answer naturally in conversation.
days.pronunciation_tip: The Spanish 'v' often sounds close to a soft 'b' — so 'vivir' (to live) sounds closer to 'bee-VEER' than an English 'v' sound.

days.title: Day 5: Polite Expressions

words.example_sentence_target: Gracias por el café. - De nada.
words.example_sentence_native: Thanks for the coffee. - You're welcome.
Excuse me, I'm going to pass through.

words.example_sentence_target: Que tengas un buen día. - Igualmente.

words.example_sentence_native: Can you help me? - Of course!

words.example_sentence_target: Nos vemos a las cinco. - Vale.

sentences.native: The food smells good. Enjoy your meal!
sentences.phonetic: ලා කො-මී-දා හුවේ-ලේ බියෙන්. බු-එන් ප්‍රො-වේ-චෝ!

lines.speaker: A
lines.native: Thank you so much for your time.

lines.speaker: A
lines.native: Dinner is ready. Enjoy your meal!
lines.phonetic: lah SEH-nah ehs-TAH LEES-tah. bwehn proh-VEH-choh

lines.speaker: A
lines.native: Shall we go to the movies tomorrow?

lines.speaker: B
lines.target: Vale, me parece una buena idea.
lines.native: Okay, sounds like a good idea.
lines.phonetic: VAH-leh, meh pah-REH-seh OO-nah BWEH-nah ee-DEH-ah

days.cultural_note: 'Buen provecho' is said before eating, similar to 'enjoy your meal' or the French 'bon appétit' — it's polite to say this to others at the table before starting to eat.
days.pronunciation_tip: The Spanish 'j' (and 'g' before e/i) makes a breathy 'h' sound, like clearing your throat gently — for example 'gracias' has a soft sound but 'jamón' starts with a stronger 'h'.

days.title: Day 6: Yes / No / Maybe

words.example_sentence_target: Exacto, eso es lo que pienso.

sentences.native: Maybe we'll see each other later.

sentences.target: Quizás no es una buena idea.
sentences.native: Perhaps it's not a good idea.
sentences.phonetic: කි-සාස් නෝ එස් උ-නා බු-ඒ-නා ඉ-දේ-ආ.

sentences.native: I don't know where it is.
You don't need to repeat it.

lines.speaker: A
lines.native: Are you coming to the party tonight?
lines.phonetic: VYEH-nehs ah lah fyehs-TAH EHS-tah NOH-cheh

lines.speaker: A
lines.target: Entonces, ¿la reunión es a las tres?
lines.native: So, the meeting is at three?
lines.phonetic: ehn-TOHN-sehs, lah reh-oo-NYOHN ehs ah lahs trehs

lines.speaker: B
lines.target: Lo siento, no entiendo. ¿Puede hablar más lento?
lines.native: Sorry, I don't understand. Can you speak slower?
lines.phonetic: loh SYEHN-toh, no ehn-TYEHN-doh. PWEH-deh ah-BLAR mahs LEHN-toh

lines.speaker: A
lines.target: La dirección es calle Sol, número 25.
lines.native: The address is Sol Street, number 25.
lines.phonetic: lah dee-rehk-SYOHN ehs KAH-yeh sol, NOO-meh-roh VEIN-teh-SEEN-koh

lines.speaker: B
lines.native: Sorry, can you repeat the number?

days.cultural_note: It's completely normal and polite in Spanish-speaking cultures to say 'No sé' (I don't know) plainly, rather than guessing — honesty about uncertainty is generally valued over pretending to be sure.
days.pronunciation_tip: The Spanish 'r' between vowels is a quick single tap of the tongue (like a soft American 'd' or 't' sound in 'butter') — different from the rolled double 'rr' sound.

days.title: Day 7: Simple Conversation Practice

words.example_sentence_native: I'm fine, thank you. And you?

words.example_sentence_target: Gracias por venir. - Gracias a ti por invitarme.
words.example_sentence_native: Thanks for coming. - Thank you for inviting me.

sentences.target: No muy bien, un poco cansado.
sentences.native: Not very well, a bit tired.
sentences.phonetic: නෝ මුයි බියෙන්, උන් පෝ-කෝ කන්-සා-දෝ.

sentences.target: Soy de Sri Lanka. Mucho gusto.
sentences.native: I'm from Sri Lanka. Nice to meet you.
sentences.phonetic: සොයි දේ ශ්‍රී ලංකා. මූ-චෝ ගුස්-තෝ.

sentences.native: I have to go. See you tomorrow.

lines.speaker: B
lines.native: My name is Rohan. And you?

dialogues.context: Asking where someone's from and how they are.

lines.speaker: A
lines.native: I'm from Spain. Where are you from?
lines.phonetic: soy deh es-PAH-nyah. deh DOHN-deh EH-rehs

lines.speaker: B
lines.target: Soy de Sri Lanka. Estoy bien, gracias.
lines.native: I'm from Sri Lanka. I'm fine, thank you.
lines.phonetic: soy deh sree LAHN-kah. ehs-TOY byehn, GRAH-syahs

lines.speaker: A
lines.target: Este es mi amigo, Juan. Mucho gusto.
lines.native: This is my friend, Juan. Nice to meet you.
lines.phonetic: EHS-teh ehs mee ah-MEE-goh, HWAHN. MOO-choh GOOS-toh

lines.speaker: B
lines.native: Delighted. Well, goodbye. See you later!

lines.speaker: B
lines.target: No sé. Lo siento. Hable más lento, por favor.
lines.native: I don't know. Sorry. Speak slower, please.
lines.phonetic: no seh. loh SYEHN-toh. AH-bleh mahs LEHN-toh, por fah-VOR

days.cultural_note: Everything you learned this week — greetings, introductions, asking how someone is, politeness, and confirmation words — comes together to form the base of your first real conversation. Practice these daily.
days.pronunciation_tip: Try reading this week's phrases out loud, all together — building fluency comes from repetition, not from memorizing rules.
